explanation.rule: Negative prefixes are added to the beginning of words to create opposite or negative meanings. The prefix un- is the most common negative prefix in English.

groups.note: Added to adjectives and some verbs to give the opposite meaning. It is the most widely used negative prefix and works with a large range of adjectives.

examples.definition: not able to be used or reached
not treating people equally or in a just way
not able to be trusted or depended on

wordForms.definition: not able to be used, obtained, or reached
wordForms.example: The manager was unavailable for comment.
wordForms.rootDefinition: ready or able to be used or obtained

wordForms.example: Some delays during the building works were unavoidable.
wordForms.rootDefinition: able to be prevented or kept away from

wordForms.definition: not sure; not clearly known or established
wordForms.example: The outcome of the vote remains uncertain.
She felt uncomfortable answering personal questions.
wordForms.rootDefinition: giving a feeling of physical ease and relaxation

wordForms.definition: not anticipated; happening as a surprise
wordForms.example: The announcement came as a completely unexpected shock.
wordForms.rootDefinition: thought likely or anticipated to happen

wordForms.example: Many employees felt the new policy was deeply unfair.
wordForms.rootDefinition: treating people equally; just and reasonable

wordForms.definition: not giving useful assistance or support
wordForms.example: The customer service assistant was rude and unhelpful.
wordForms.rootDefinition: making it easier to do something; useful

wordForms.example: It is unlikely that the project will finish on time.
wordForms.rootDefinition: probable; having a good chance of happening

wordForms.definition: not needed; more than is required
wordForms.example: Please avoid making unnecessary noise in the library.

wordForms.definition: not able to be trusted or depended on
wordForms.example: The old bus service was unreliable and frequently late.
wordForms.rootDefinition: consistently good and able to be trusted

multipleChoiceExercises.sentence: The service was ___ after the technical fault occurred.
Some noise during construction is, unfortunately, ___.
The weather forecast for the weekend is still ___.
Many workers considered the new pay cuts completely ___.
It is ___ that the results will be announced before Friday.
He apologised for causing ___ stress during the handover.

transformExercises.sentence: The roads were ___ during the flooding. (AVAILABLE)
Some risks are ___ in a competitive business environment. (AVOID)
The website was ___ and kept crashing at critical moments. (RELIABLE)
He found the long silence at the dinner table very ___. (COMFORTABLE)
The success of the small local team was completely ___. (EXPECT)
